channel.title: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD)
channel.description: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD) generated by script

item.title: Iran Intensifies Strikes on Gulf Energy Facilities
item.description: The White House has convinced Israel not to attack South Pars gas field again—so long as Tehran does not target Qatar.

item.title: Investors Are in Denial About the Economic Impact of the Iran War
item.description: The damage to energy infrastructure in the Gulf will have enduring impact.

item.title: The Follies of Predicting War
item.description: A new book debates who wins wars and why.

item.title: Why U.S. Victory in Iran Would Be Bad for Washington—and the World
item.description: The possibility of Trump imposing his personal whims on another nation is even more frightening than U.S. failure.

item.title: What Trump May Do if He Loses in Iran
item.description: The president’s go-to playbook in the face of defeat would be especially dangerous in the context of war.

item.title: Starlink Has Privatized Geopolitics
item.description: From Ukraine to Iran, Elon Musk’s service has become an arbiter of foreign policy.

item.title: Is playing music good for the brain?
item.description: It would seem so, even for amateurs

item.title: How to Get Money for Cold Sports in Warm Places
item.description: Winter Olympic success brings funding hopes to Southern Hemisphere athletes.

item.title: Sex, Liberation, and Obsession in 1970s Istanbul
item.description: “The Museum of Innocence” is a nostalgic and unnerving adaptation of Orhan Pamuk’s work.

item.title: Cover Story newsletter: Operation Blind Fury
item.description: An exclusive look at how we designed this week’s cover

item.title: Were Foreign Nationals and Myanmar Rebel Groups Plotting an Attack Against India?
item.description: The arrest of seven foreign citizens under UAPA drew attention to Myanmar-based EAOs and their links to India – but media reports thus far have obscured more thanthey have illuminated.

item.title: Iran Is Sanctioning America
item.description: By choking off the Strait of Hormuz to oil flowing from pro-US Persian Gulf countries, while still shipping a substantial amount of its own oil to China, Iran has effectively slapped painful sanctions on the US. If these de facto sanctions last, they will impose high economic costs on many Americans – and political costs on Donald Trump.

item.title: Against the Odds: How Takaichi Turned Her 2nd Meeting With Trump Into a Success
item.description: Takaichi avoided making concrete security commitments in the Iran war, while also garnering Trump's praise.

item.title: Tashiev Returns to Kyrgyzstan, Questioned as a ‘Witness’
item.description: That Tashiev is being questioned as a witness, rather than detained as a culprit, is a political hedge.

item.title: Africa Is Forging Its Own Green Future
item.description: Despite bearing the brunt of a climate crisis it did not create, Africa is being held back by a financial system that has long failed to meet its needs. But instead of waiting for outside solutions, African institutions are laying the groundwork for a green transition that advances both development and climate resilience.

item.title: South Asia’s LNG Strategy Was Built for the Last Crisis – Not This One
item.description: Bangladesh, Pakistan, and Sri Lanka spent years signing long-term Gulf LNG contracts to avoid the volatility of spot markets. The Iran war tore those plans to shreds.

item.title: Italy’s Referendum Could Be Meloni’s Biggest Test Yet
item.description: The Iran war has raised the stakes of the vote for the prime minister.

item.title: Will China’s Stalinesque Purges Sabotage Its Military Ambitions?
item.description: A side-by-side comparison of Stalin’s own military purges can help answer that question.

item.title: Continuity Under Fire
item.description: Why the Gulf’s grand strategies will not change.

item.title: Asia’s Invisible Workforce in the Middle East: The Geopolitics of Migrant Labor Safety
item.description: As war threatens the Gulf, millions of Asian migrant workers face hidden dangers, reminding governments that human lives cannot be an afterthought in economic progress.

item.title: Is the Private Credit Boom Going Bust?
item.description: Since 2008, private credit has expanded into a $3.5 trillion industry, driven by private equity and operating outside the reach of traditional banking rules. As cracks begin to appear, the risks embedded in a system built on opaque valuations and rising leverage are becoming harder to ignore.

item.title: China’s ‘Terminator’ Info Op and the Future of AI
item.description: China is stoking U.S. fears of world-ending AI – despite having no qualms about fast-tracking the technology in its own military.

item.title: Takaichi’s Calculated Gamble at the Trump Summit Amid the Iran Crisis
item.description: Trumps said that Tokyo is “really stepping up to the plate” on Iran – “unlike NATO.”

item.title: Telling Americans the Truth About Immigration
item.description: Donald Trump’s mass deportations are raising the prices of critical goods and services, gutting a key driver of innovation, and eliminating just about the only force working to rein in the national debt. With immigrants less likely to commit crimes than native-born Americans, to call his crackdown misguided is an understatement.

item.title: Trump’s Cuba Plans Create Tension
item.description: The White House wants a win. Many Cubans are pushing for democratization.

item.title: Silence Can Be an Epic Folly: A Response to Shashi Tharoor
item.description: One can stand up for principle without adopting a confrontational posture. For a country that prizes strategic autonomy, this is precisely the moment to exercise it.

item.title: Selective Outrage Won’t End the Iran War
item.description: The United Nations Security Council’s resolution condemning Iran’s retaliatory strikes on US bases and energy facilities in neighboring countries ignores why they occurred. The resolution is less about international law than about protecting oil flows, global economic stability, and the Gulf states’ privileged status.

item.title: The Real Fallout From Trump’s Tariffs
item.description: Donald Trump might have hoped that his “Liberation Day” tariffs would punish China and boost the competitiveness of American industries, but they have instead had the opposite effect. Worse, the policy has had far-reaching unintended consequences that few policymakers and commentators have yet to reckon with.

item.title: Why Pakistan Is Desperate to Avert an Iran-Saudi Confrontation
item.description: Under a bilateral defense agreement, Islamabad will have to support Riyadh in the event of an Iran-Saudi showdown. That is not in Pakistan’s interest.

item.title: Singapore’s 2026 Budget: A Good Time for Surpluses
item.description: The city-state's fiscal prudence puts it in a good position to weather the current volatility in the global oil market.

item.title: Southeast Asia’s Railway Expansion
item.description: China dominates for now, but there are opportunities for Western players.

item.title: Dhaka Must Tread Cautiously on ‘Routine Defense Agreements’ with the US
item.description: Hosting American bases will not make Bangladesh secure. Rather, it could undermine its sovereignty and draw it into someone else’s wars.

item.title: Could Vietnam Help Repair U.S.-Cuba Relations?
item.description: As a communist nation and a responsible member of the international community, Vietnam is a natural bridge between Washington and Havana.

item.title: Malaysia Has Not Informed US of Withdrawal From Trade Deal: Official
item.description: The status of the deal has been unclear since the Malaysia's trade minister said that last month's U.S. Supreme Court ruling rendered the agreement "void."

item.title: There’s a Reason No President Before Trump Authorized War With Iran
item.description: How this war ends is as uncertain as the reasons for starting it.

item.title: How Iran Sees the War
item.description: External escalation, internal consolidation.

item.title: Trump, Xi, and the Case for Strategic Calm
item.description: How America can take advantage of the status quo.

item.title: The Two Israels
item.description: A socioeconomic divide shapes the country’s politics—and its aggressive foreign Policy.

item.title: In Southeast Asia, the Scramble for Energy Is On
item.description: With each nation exposed in different ways to global oil markets, the regional impact of the price shock is likely to be significant, but uneven.

item.title: Caught Between Pakistan and the Iran War: What Comes Next for Afghanistan?
item.description: To both the east and the west, the nation's major trade routes are obstructed by conflict.
